title: Evaluating Transformer NER
concept: Evaluate NER using token-level precision and recall, paying attention to entity boundary detection errors.

exercises.prompt: Before we evaluate it, what does the NLP task 'NER' stand for?
exercises.explain: NER stands for Named Entity Recognition, which is the task of finding and labeling specific names, places, and organizations in text.

cards.title: Close, But No Cigar!
cards.body: Imagine your AI reads the phrase "New York Times" and extracts only "New York" as a city, completely missing that the whole phrase is a newspaper. How do we measure and grade these near-miss boundary mistakes? Let's find out!

cards.title: Evaluating NER
cards.body: To evaluate a Named Entity Recognition (NER) model, we count how many entity boundaries (like the start and end of a name) it got right. We calculate Precision (how many of its guesses were correct) and Recall (how many of the actual entities it successfully found).

cards.title: The Security Guard
cards.body: Imagine evaluating a security guard at an airport check-in. If they flag every single bag as dangerous, they won't miss any actual weapons (100% Recall), but they'll cause a huge line of innocent, angry travelers (0% Precision). Similarly, a model evaluating text needs to find the actual names (Recall) without flagging normal words as names by mistake (Precision).

cards.title: Boundary Errors
cards.body:
Let's look at the sentence: "I visited San Francisco."

- Actual Name: "San Francisco" (1 entity)
- Model Guess: "San" (1 entity)

Because the model cut the boundary short and missed "Francisco", this counts as a mistake! Both precision and recall drop because the exact boundary was missed.

cards.title: The Accuracy Trap
cards.body: People often think that if a model gets 99% accuracy on individual tokens, it's doing amazing. But since most words in a book are NOT names (they are just normal words), a model could get 99% accuracy by simply guessing that *nothing* is a name. That's why we use Precision and Recall instead!

cards.title: Precision vs. Recall
cards.body: See how precision and recall track different types of model mistakes.

right.text: Focuses on quantity. Of all the real names in the text, how many did the model find? Prevents missed names.

left.text: Focuses on quality. Of all the names the model guessed, how many were actually correct? Prevents false alarms.

exercises.prompt: If a model labels 10 words as names, but only 8 of those are actually names, what is the Precision?
exercises.explain: If the model flags 10 words as names, but only 8 are correct, its Precision is 8 out of 10, which is 80%.

exercises.prompt: If a model extracts 'Apple' instead of 'Apple Inc.', this is considered an entity boundary error.
exercises.explain: Boundary errors occur when a model includes too many or too few words in an entity (e.g., extracting "New York" instead of "New York City").

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing evaluation term:
exercises.sentence: The metric that measures how many of the actual names in the text the model successfully found is called [blank].
exercises.explain: Recall measures the model's ability to find all the actual entities in the text without missing them.

exercises.prompt: For the text "Harry Potter", Model A labels "Harry" and "Potter" as two separate person entities. Model B labels "Harry Potter" as one single person entity. Which is correct?

options.text: Model A is perfect because it found all the words.
Model B is better because it correctly grouped the words into one entity.

exercises.explain: Model B got the boundaries exactly right. Model A split a single entity into two separate ones, which is a boundary error.

exercises.prompt: Your Transformer model scans a sentence containing 5 actual names. It flags 4 words as names, but only 3 of those flagged words are correct. What are your Precision and Recall scores?
exercises.explain: Precision measures how many flagged entities were actually correct (3/4 = 75%). Recall measures how many of the actual true entities we found (3/5 = 60%). Finding 3 correct names out of 5 total true names means we missed 2, leading to 60% Recall.
